coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive fluorescence probe for methyl parathion detection in vegetable and fruit samples based on N and S co-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Lingfei
Qian, Minjie
Sun, Huijuan
Yang, Zhen-quan
Xiao, Lixia
Gong, Xiaojuan
Hu, Qin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a highly sensitive fluorescence sensing system based on nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) was for the first time developed for indirect measurement of methyl parathion (MP) with the aid of alkaline-induced hydrolysis. The N,S-CDs were prepared by using o-phenylenediamine and l-cysteine as precursors via hydrothermal heating. In a strong alkaline environment, MP was hydrolysed to p-NP which can suppress the fluorescence of N,S-CDs via a combination of IFE and dynamic interaction. The N,S-CDs exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity towards MP sensing, achieving a linear range of 0.01–15.0 μg/mL and a detection limit as low as 1.56 ng/mL. The proposed sensing platform was applied for MP detection in vegetable and fruit samples with satisfactory results. The recoveries were in a range of 96.4–104.4 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 3.02 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methyl parathion
Carbon dots
Food analysis
Fluorescence method

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of real food samples by N,S-CDs
Selectivity and anti-interference of the proposed fluorescence sensing system
Detection of MP by the proposed fluorescence sensing system
Mechanism of the established detection system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) particle size distribution histogram and (C) FTIR spectrum of N,S-CDs.

(A) The UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CDs and (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs under different λex. The N,S-CDs solution (0.3 mg/mL) was prepared in double distilled water.
The effects of (A) pH of reaction system, (B) reaction temperature, (C) reaction time and (D) N,S-CDs concentration on MP detection.
The (A) Selectivity and (B) anti-interference ability of N,S-CDs towards MP detection.

(A) The influence of MP concentration on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs. 1‒12: 0.0, 0.1, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0, 30.0 and 40.0 μg/mL of MP. (B) The linear relationship between F0/F and MP concentration. The N,S-CDs solution (0.3 mg/mL) was prepared in PBS buffer (200 mM, pH 12.0).
(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of p-NP, and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CDs. (B) The fluorescence decay curves of N,S-CDs in the absence and in the presence of p-NP.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of N,S-CDs and the principle for MP detection.
Comparative results of the analytical performance of various NPs-based fluorescence methods for MP detection.

row.entry: Peach, cucumber, pear, cabbage, cherry tomato

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results of MP detection in real food samples by using the proposed N,S-CDs-based fluorescence method and HPLC.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive fluorescence probe for methyl parathion detection in vegetable and fruit samples based on N and S co-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a highly sensitive fluorescence sensing system based on nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) was for the first time developed for indirect measurement of methyl parathion (MP) with the aid of alkaline-induced hydrolysis. The N,S-CDs were prepared by using o-phenylenediamine and l-cysteine as precursors via hydrothermal heating. In a strong alkaline environment, MP was hydrolysed to p-NP which can suppress the fluorescence of N,S-CDs via a combination of IFE and dynamic interaction. The N,S-CDs exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity towards MP sensing, achieving a linear range of 0.01–15.0 μg/mL and a detection limit as low as 1.56 ng/mL. The proposed sensing platform was applied for MP detection in vegetable and fruit samples with satisfactory results. The recoveries were in a range of 96.4–104.4 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 3.02 %.

ce:section.ce:para: Methyl parathion (MP), a type of organophosphate pesticides (OPs), has been extensively applied in agricultural industry to prevent crops from insect attack (Liang et al., 2014; Garcia et al., 2003). However, the cumulative use and bioaccumulation of MP has attracted increasing concerns on food safety due to its risk to the human body and environment. It is reported that the enrichment of MP in human body would lead to serious disruption to mammal nervous system, causing headaches, nausea, night-waking, diarrhea, restlessness, difficulty breathing, dizziness and abdominal cramps (Edward and Tchounwou, 2005; Rubin et al., 2002). To make matters worse, MP can even cause damage to human nervous system, leading to organ dysfunction and ultimately death (Lan et al., 2019). With the above information, the development of a sensitive, reliable and convenient analytical method for MP detection is extremely important for food safety monitoring.

Up till now, a series of methods have been developed for sensitive and selective MP detection, including electrochemical detection (Silva et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021), biosensor (Senbua et al., 2020), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Chen et al., 2018), enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Kumar et al., 2005), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC/MS) (da Silva et al., 2015) and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Xie et al., 2020). These methods have some merits in favourable sensitivity, adequate selectivity and good accuracy; however, most of them suffer from inevitable disadvantages of time-consuming process, expensive instrumentation and requirement of skillful operation, which limit their real application scope. In recent years, the usage of quantum dots (QDs)-based fluorescence methods for MP detection was found to be a good choice for the problems above (Fahimi -Kashani and Hormozi-Nezhad, 2015; Yan et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2019). The fluorescence QDs probes showed a mass of advantages like easy operation, high sensitivity, good selectivity and high accuracy.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a newly discovered type of nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm (Baker and Baker, 2010). The CDs were reported to exhibit excellent advantages in low cost of fabrication, good water solubility, low toxicity, unique optical properties and excellent biocompatibility (Nekoueian et al., 2019). Recent studies showed that the CDs could serves as sensitive fluorescence nanoprobes for MP detection. For example, Wei et al. developed a fluorescence sensing system based on N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for MP detection by using methyl parathion hydrolase (MPH) as a catalyst for MP hydrolysis (Song et al., 2017). Hou et al. reported a fluorescence sensing platform based on l-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots (Tyr-CDs) to detect MP by using tyrosinase as a catalyst for the oxidation of tyrosine methyl ester on CDs surface. Among these reports, MPH and tyrosinase are common enzymes to construct CDs-based methods. However, the enzymes are relatively expensive and their activity could be inhibited under different solvent composition and solution conditions (Hou et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2019). Thus, it is highly necessary to develop enzyme-free fluorescence methods to detect MP in a simple and economic way. Yet, so far there are rare research in this area with only one case was reported, where a fluorescence sensor based on N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was reported to indirectly measure MP with the alkaline-induced hydrolysis, achieving a detection limit of 1.87 ng/mL (1.87 ppb) (Lan et al., 2019). In the light of above-mentioned reasons, more enzyme-free sensors based on CDs are expected to be developed for MP detection.

In this work, a fluorescence sensing platform based on nitrogen and sulphur doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) was constructed for the indirect detection of MP with the assistance of alkaline-induced hydrolysis. The N,S-CDs was prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal heating of o-phenylenediamine and l-cysteine. Under strong alkaline environment, the MP can be hydrolysed to p-NP in the absence of any other enzymes or reagents (Lan et al., 2019), where the p-NP could quench the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. Based on the quenching effect, a new analytical method for MP detection with simple operation, low cost and high sensitivity was established. The synthetic procedure of N,S-CDs and the detection principle for MP was illustrated in Scheme 1 . The pH value of the reaction system, reaction temperature, reaction time and N,S-CDs concentration were optimised. The linear range, sensitivity, selectivity and anti-interference ability of the sensing system were evaluated, and the mechanism of action was studied. Finally, the feasibility of the proposed method was studied by applying it for MP detection in vegetable and fruit samples.

The o-phenylenediamine, l-cysteine and p-nitrophenol (p-NP) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methyl parathion (MP) and the other pesticides were obtained from Beijing Manhage Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents were in analytical purity and used without further purification. All solutions were prepared by using double distilled water purified by a Milli-Q plus system (Millipore S.A., Molsheim, France). The real samples were purchased from local markets at Yangzhou City, China.

The N,S-CDs was fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and l-cysteine as precursors according to a previously reported method with slight modification (Yue et al. 2021). In detail, 0.108 g o-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 20.0 mL double distilled water and then 0.121 g l-cysteine was added to mix. After being incubated at room temperature for 15 min, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (30.0 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The reaction product was centrifuged for 15 min at 8000 rpm to remove the large particles after being naturally cooled down to room temperature. The final pale-yellow solution was freeze-dried to collect dry N,S-CDs product.

Unless otherwise stated, the N,S-CDs solution was prepared at a concentration of 0.3 mg/mL by dissolving dry N,S-CDs powder in PBS buffer (200 mM, pH 12.0). The linearity of the studied method was assessed by recording the changes of the fluorescence intensity of a N,S-CDs solution (0.3 mg/mL) after adding various contents of MP. Firstly, the fluorescence intensity of a 2.0 mL N,S-CDs solution (0.3 mg/mL) was recorded as F0. Then, different concentrations of MP were continuously added into the N,S-CDs solution, making the added contents of MP in the N,S-CDs solution in a range of 0.01–40.0 μg/mL. Then, the mixture was incubated at 80 °C for 5 min to ensure the reaction was complete. After being naturally cooled down to room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was recorded as F. The λex and λem were set as 350 nm and 407 nm, respectively. The calibration curves were obtained by plotting the fluorescence quenching efficiency F0/F against the concentration of MP.

To assess the selectivity of the studied method, a series of pesticides with similar chemical structures to MP, including methomyl, phoxim, dimethoate, chlorpyrifos, diazinon, acephate, atrazine, acetaminophen, pyrimethanil, pymetrozine, hymexazol, thiabendazole, acetamiprid, fluazinam, glyphosate and 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), were respectively mixed with 2.0 mL N,S-CDs solutions (0.3 mg/mL, pH 12.0). The added content of each pesticide was 3.0 μg/mL. The fluorescence intensity F0 and F were measured following the reaction and detection procedure mentioned above.

The anti-interference ability of the studied method was also assessed. The fluorescence intensity of a 2.0 mL N,S-CDs solution (0.3 mg/mL) was firstly measured and recorded as F0. Then, MP was mixed at a concentration of 3.0 μg/mL. After that, different kinds of the other pesticides was add to mix, and the added concentration of each pesticide was 3.0 μg/mL. The mixture was incubated at 80 °C for 5 min and then naturally cooled down to room temperature. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture after reaction was measured and recorded as F. The λex and λem were set as 350 nm and 407 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of real food samples by N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Five kinds of vegetables and fruits, including peach, cucumber, pear, cabbage and cherry tomato, were purchased from the local markets. For the sample treatment, the cabbage was directly cut into pieces for further extraction whereas the treatment of peach, cucumber, pear and cherry tomato was relatively complex. The peels of peach, cucumber, pear and cherry tomato were firstly scraped and then cut into pieces before extraction. Afterwards, 5.0 g of each sample was weighed and extracted by soaking with 10.0 mL acetone overnight. Finally, 20.0 μL of the extractions was added into a 2.0 mL N,S-CDs solution (0.3 mg/mL), and incubated at 80 °C for 5 min. To avoid water evaporation and keep the concentration of N,S-CDs constant, the reaction mixture was heated in a 5.0 mL PTFE centrifuge tube with a covered lid. The F0 and F were recorded with the λex and λem setting as 350 nm and 407 nm, respectively. In addition, in order to check the measurement accuracy of the studied method, the standard solutions with known contents of MP were spiked into the sample extractions.
The samples were also analysed by HPLC method for comparison and the experimental details were deposited in supplementary material.

The morphology and size information of N,S-CDs were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 1 A, the N,S-CDs species were in spherical structure and were evenly dispersed on the TEM grid. The particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 1B) obtained by randomly counting about 80 particles with a Nano Measurer software (v1.2) showed that the sizes of N,S-CDs species were in a range of 1.72–3.66 nm with an average diameter of 2.95 ± 0.3 nm.

The surface functional information of N,S-CDs were characterised by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 1C, a broad absorption peak observed at 3108 cm−1 is corresponding to NH and OH bonds. Another broad absorption peak observed at 1921 cm−1 belongs to SH bonds. The strong absorption peaks at 1628 cm−1 and 1374 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CN bonds. The absorption peaks corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO and SO bonds were observed at 1273 cm−1 and 1025 cm−1, respectively. The absorption peaks at 751 cm−1 and 612 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of CS bond. The elemental composition of the as-synthesised CDs was studied by elemental analysis (Table. S1). The CDs comprised of 59.55 % carbon (C), 5.74 % hydrogen (H), 10.41 % oxygen (O), 18.44 % nitrogen (N) and 5.86 % sulphur (S). In essence, the above results indicate that N and S were successfully doped into CDs.

The absorption property of N,S-CDs was characterised by UV–vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 A (blue curve), the UV–vis spectrum of N,S-CDs exhibited two typical absorption peaks at 230 nm and 270 nm, which are caused by the π→π* transition of SP2 hybrid carbon and the n→π* transition of CO bond, respectively (Li et al., 2018). The fluorescence property of N,S-CDs was characterised by PL spectroscopy. The excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs showed an excitation peak at 350 nm (Fig. 2A, red curve). The emission spectrum of N,S-CDs (Fig. 2A, black curve) showed an emission peak at 407 nm with the excitation at 350 nm. The N,S-CDs solution emitted bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (Inset of Fig. 2A). Fig. 2B showed the fluorescence emission spectrum of N,S-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (λex). When the λex increased from 300 nm to 430 nm, the fluorescence emission peak of N,S-CDs varied in a range of 390–490 nm, showing typical λex-dependent PL behaviour, which was considered to be the result of different emission sites on the surface of CDs (Zhang et al., 2020). With quinine sulphate as reference, the quantum yield (QY) of N,S-CDs was determined to be 3.9 % (Fig. S1).

The working parameters including pH of the reaction system, reaction temperature, reaction time and the concentration of N,S-CDs that are important influential factors to the analytical performance of the sensing system (Hu et al., 2019; Lan et al., 2019) were optimised in detail.

Firstly, the effect of the pH of the reaction system on MP detection was studied. The pH not only affects the N,S-CDs fluorescence intensity, but also influences the hydrolysis efficiency of MP to p-NP. The PBS buffer (200 mM) at a pH range of 2.0–12.0 were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 A, no obvious fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs was observed in the pH range of 2.0–10.0. When pH > 10, a continuous increase of the fluorescence quenching efficiency F0/F with a dramatic increase of F0/F at pH 12 was observed. This is due to the fact that MP can only be efficiently hydrolysed at a strong alkaline environment (Lan et al., 2019). The hydrolysis efficiency of MP was low at pH ≤ 10 whereas in a strong alkaline environment (pH > 10), the hydrolysis efficiency of MP increases with pH and more MP was concerted to p-NP, leading to a dramatic increase of F0/F at pH 12. To confirm the conversion of MP to p-NP at an alkaline solution, the mass spectra of MP, p-NP and the hydrolysis product of MP at pH 12 (working condition) were obtained (Fig. S2). The mass spectra of MP (Fig. S2A) and p-NP (Fig. S2B) revealed the characteristic mass ion peaks at m/z 285.9900 and 138.0203, respectively. On the mass spectrum of MP hydrolysis product (Fig. S2C) at pH 12, a mass peak at m/z 138.0203 assigned to a p-NP mass ion was observed, which confirms the conversion of MP to p-NP. Based on the above results, pH 12.0 was chosen for the following experiment.

Next, the effect of reaction temperature on MP detection was studied (Fig. 3B) since reaction temperature is an important parameter that governs the hydrolysis efficiency of MP to p-NP. When the temperature was increased from 30 °C to 80 °C, F0/F gradually improved and kept stable when the temperature was higher than 80 °C. Therefore, 80 °C was used as the optimal reaction temperature for the subsequent sensing work.

Then, the effect of reaction time on MP detection was investigated. Fig. 3C showed that the F0/F reached almost stable at 5.0 min, which means that the reaction was almost completed within 5.0 min. As such, 5.0 min was selected as the optimal response time for this work.

Finally, the concentration of N,S-CDs on MP detection was studied. Fig. 3D showed that the F0/F fluctuated with the change of N,S-CDs concentration. When the concentration of N,S-CDs was 0.3 mg/mL, the detection system achieved the best performance. Consequently, the optimal concentration of the N,S-CDs solution was selected as 0.3 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of the proposed fluorescence sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the N,S-CDs-based fluorescence sensing system towards MP were examined. Fig. 4 A shows the effect of interfering substances on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs. The fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs in presence of various pesticides were deposited as Fig. S3. It was observed that only MP caused obvious fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs while the other examined pesticides (including methomyl, phoxim, dimethoate, chlorpyrifos, diazinon, acephate, atrazine, acetaminophen, pyrimethanil, pymetrozine, hymexazol, thiabendazole, acetamiprid, fluazinam, glyphosate and 2,4-D), which are similar in structure to MP, did not have apparent influence on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs. It indicated that the proposed fluorescence sensing system has excellent selectivity ability towards MP. To study the absorbance behavior of N,S-CDs before and after adding MP and other pesticides, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-CDs in the presence of various kinds of pesticides were acquired. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the characteristic absorption peaks of each added pesticide can be observed after they were introduced into the N,S-CDs solution. Fig. 4B shows the effect of interfering substances on the fluorescence intensity of a N,S-CDs/MP mixture. The fluorescence emission spectra of a N,S-CDs/MP mixture in presence of various pesticides were deposited as Fig. S5. It can be seen that the fluorescence quenching efficiency F0/F caused by MP did not change significantly after the introduction of the above mentioned interfering substances, which indicated that the good anti-interference ability of the N,S-CDs-based fluorescence sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MP by the proposed fluorescence sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of a N,S-CDs solution (0.3 mg/mL) containing different concentrations of MP were recorded in Fig. 5 A. It was observed that as the content of MP increased, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs decreased proportionally, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs by MP. The F0/F showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of MP in a range of 0.01–15.0 μg/mL with a linear equation: F0/F = 0.2492[C]+1.042, R2 = 0.9919) following the typical Stern-Volmer equation, where [C] refers to the concentration of MP. The detection limit calculated by the three times standard deviation rule (3σ rule) was determined to be 1.56 ng/mL.

In addition, as the content of MP increases, the fluorescence of N,S-CDs was gradually quenched and the λem of N,S-CDs underwent gradual red shifts. This phenomenon was reported to be due to two possible reasons. One is that the addition of a quencher could combine with the functional groups attached to the CDs surface, causing changes of the functional groups on CDs surface (Gong et al., 2017). Another one is that the addition of a quencher interacts with CDs and produces new substances (Song et al., 2019). To explore whether the red shift of the N,S-CDs fluorescence spectrum is due to the formation new substance after the addition of MP, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of MP were acquired in Fig. S6. It can be seen that when MP was added to the N,S-CDs solution, the characteristic absorption peak of p-NP (hydrolysis product of MP) appeared at 400 nm; however, no shift in the position of the peak was observed with the increase of the MP concentration, indicating that no new substance was produced. As such, the red shift of the N,S-CDs fluorescence spectrum after the addition of MP was most likely due to the reason that hydrolysis product of MP, i.e., p-NP, combined with the functional groups on CDs surface and caused the changes of the CDs surface functional groups.

A comparison of the analytical performance of our proposed method and the other NPs-based fluorescence sensing systems was showed in Table 1 . It can be seen that the detection limit was lower than the previously reported NPs-based fluorescence method, indicating the high sensitivity of our proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the established detection system
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism was based on the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs. In general, the p-NP can quench the fluorescence of N,S-CDs by either Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) (Shi et al., 2015) or IFE (Bian et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2015). The FRET sensing system involves the intermolecular interaction between a chemosensor and a target molecule, and it only works when they are at a particular distance or they have a complementary geometry; the IFE works if there is overlapping between the absorption band of an absorber and the excitation and/or emission band of a fluorophore to some extent (Zheng et al., 2013).

To study if the interaction between p-NP and N,S-CDs is a result of FRET or IFE, the UV–vis spectrum of p-NP (black curve), the excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs (blue curve) and the emission spectrum of N,S-CDs (red curve) were studied. As shown by Fig. 6 A, p-NP had an absorption peak at 400 nm while the excitation peak and emission peak of N,S-CDs were at 350 nm and 407 nm, respectively. Obviously, the absorption spectrum of p-NP well overlapped with the emission spectrum of N,S-CDs and partially overlapped with the excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching effect of p-NP on N,S-CDs fluorescence was a result of inner filter effect (IFE).

The quenching mechanism can also be divided into static quenching and dynamic quenching, where the former one will not change the fluorescence lifetime of a fluorophore while the later one will cause the decrease of the fluorescence lifetime of a fluorophore (Bian et al., 2018). To further investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence decay curves of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs/p-NP mixture were acquired (Fig. 6B). The fitted corresponding lifetimes were shown in Table S2. The average lifetimes of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs/p-NP mixture were 2.36 ns and 1.78 ns, respectively, indicating that the addition of p-NP resulted in a decrease of the average lifetime of N,S-CDs. This implied the dynamic interaction between N,S-CDs and p-NP. As such, the quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs by p-NP was a combined result of IFE and dynamic interaction.

To investigate the feasibility of the proposed analytical method for real sample analyses, the method was used to detect MP in five kinds of vegetables and fruits (peach, cucumber, pear, cabbage and cherry tomato) which are easily to be accessed in the market. In addition, the MP has been widely used to prevent them from insect attack in the planting industry of these vegetables and fruits (Liang et al., 2014; Garcia et al., 2003). As shown in Table 2 , no MP was detected in the vegetables and fruits samples using the fluorescence method based on N,S-CDs. The spiked quantitative recoveries ranged from 96.4 to 104.4% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 3.02 %, indicating that the established method had high accuracy. The food sample extractions were also analysed by HPLC to examine the accuracy of the fluorescence sensing system. The chromatographs of the MP standard were displayed in Fig. S7A. It was observed that there is a good linear relationship over a concentration range of 0.3–10.0 μM (Fig. S7B). No MP was found in the food samples which were in consistent with the results obtained by the N,S-CDs based method, which ambiguously demonstrated the high accuracy of the proposed fluorescence method.

In summary, an ultra-sensitive fluorescence method was established for the efficient detection of MP based on alkaline-induced hydrolysis with N,S-CDs as nanoprobe. The fluorescence N,S-CDs was efficiently suppressed by p-NP, the hydrolysis product of MP, as a result of IFE and dynamic interaction. A linear detection range of 0.01–15.0 μg/mL and a detection limit as low as 1.56 ng/mL were achieved, indicating the high sensitivity of the proposed analytical method. In addition, the proposed method showed merits in rapidity, great simplicity, low cost, high selectivity and excellent anti-interference ability. The proposed sensing platform was successfully used for the detection of MP in various vegetables and fruits samples with the recoveries ranged from 96.4 %–104.4 % and the RSDs less than 3.02 %. The research results of this work indicated the feasibility of N,S-CDs-based fluorescence nanoprobe for MP detection in complex food matrices.
No data was used for the research described in the article.

Qin Hu and Lixia Xiao designed the experiments and wrote the manuscript. Qin Hu, Lingfei Liu, and Minjie Qian performed the experiments. Qin Hu, Huijuan Sun, Zhen-quan Yang, and Xiaojuan Gong analysed the data. All the authors reviewed and approved the entire manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from 2020 Postgraduate Research and Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province, China (SJCX20_1383), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20200949), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671625), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922202), Natural Science Fund for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province, China (19KJB150042), Yangzhou Key R&D Project (Social Development), China (2020) (YZ2020063), LvYang Golden Phoenix Plan, Yangzhou City (2019) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfca.2021.104374.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Baker and Baker, 2010

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent carbon nanodots: emergent nanolights
Angewandte Chemie-Int. Ed.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Baker SN, Baker GA. Luminescent Carbon Nanodots: Emergent Nanolights. Angewandte Chemie-International Edition. 49:6726-6744.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bian et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of curcumin
Luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bian W, Wang X, Wang Y, Yang H, Huang J, Cai Z, Choi MMF. Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of curcumin. Luminescence. 33:174-180.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chen et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of an acryloyl beta-cyclodextrin-silica hybrid monolithic column and its application in pipette tip solid-phase extraction and HPLC analysis of methyl parathion and fenthion
J. Sep. Sci.
